clean_story:
Precision under palm trees. A lab where Einstein taught.

The name Caltech is short for California Institute of Technology—but it’s less a school than a factory for breakthroughs. Founded in 1891 as a vocational school, it became a physics powerhouse after Robert Millikan measured the electron’s charge in 1909 using nothing but oil drops and a stopwatch. His experiment was like weighing a single grain of sand by watching it fall through honey.

Caltech’s campus is small (124 acres) but dense with firsts: the first man-made earthquake (1930s, to test buildings), the first DNA synthesized in a test tube (1960s), and the Jet Propulsion Laboratory, which NASA uses to land rovers on Mars. Students here don’t just study rockets—they build them. Undergrads have launched over 100 satellites, some no bigger than a loaf of bread.

The rules are simple: No grades until junior year. Classes are pass/fail. The focus? Solving problems no one else can. Like when Richard Feynman cracked safe after safe at Los Alamos during the Manhattan Project—not for espionage, but because he found the locks annoying.

Three related subjects are MIT’s secret labs, how tiny satellites work, and why palm trees line tech campuses.
